data.title: Intellia Therapeutics: A Worst-Case Risk Becomes A Tragic Reality (Downgrade)
data.description:
It happened!

I’m referring to a risk factor — the worst possible risk factor — coming to pass.

Biotech and pharmaceuticals are risky businesses. We know that.

These companies spend a fortune to develop medical treatments. Many flop. Often it happens in the lab — before they’re tried on humans. Many others fail in trial. Some don’t work on humans. Some work, but no better than other already-available treatments. Others show promise but falter due to unacceptable side effects.

And these risks come on top of the usual assortment of business risks. How many patients are afflicted with the relevant condition? Will physicians prescribe it? Can patients afford it? Will insurance cover it? How does it compare to competitive treatments? For early-stage R&D-oriented firms, can they survive long enough to make money?

We in the investment community understand and have become accustomed to coping with such risks.

We diversify beyond biotech-pharma. We manage position sizes so no single stock will kill our portfolios. We diversify within the field by owing several such stocks. We really, really diversify within the field by owning specialty ETFs such as the State Street SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) or, higher on the risk scale, something like the Virtus LifeSci Clinical Trials ETF (BBC).

Sometimes, though, and fortunately very rarely, the ultimate disaster happens. I’m referring a participant in a drug trial losing his life.

Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ: NTLA) recently got hit with…

A Worst-Case Outcome

A participant in one of its trials passed away.

We do not now know, and we certainly cannot now say that Nex-z, the treatment NTLA was testing, caused his death.

It’s still early days. At present, the focus remains on fact-finding.

Not surprisingly, analysts vigorously questioned CEO John Leonard about causation during a call held on 10/27/25 to initially discuss the tragedy, and again during an earnings call on 11/7/25.

Equally unsurprising, Leonard could not on either occasion supply definitive answers. I don’t blame him for that. It’s still too early.

We cannot expect any responsible CEO to speak definitively given the currently-skimpy state of available knowledge. Doing so would have been reckless. And Leonard is to be applauded for refusing to go that route. He’ll speak further when more information becomes available. That’s the proper stance.

Wall Street traders and investors play by different rules. They are allowed to act as if the trial caused the death and as if NTLA will be seriously damaged. The Street doesn’t need facts. All it needs is aversion to uncertainty. And with NTLA right now, it has plenty of that.

Still, let’s try to make as much sense of the situation as we can.

What We Know

(1) Key Terminology — Jargon

If you invest in biotech (or pharma), it helps a lot if you really love jargon. If not, you should cultivate at least a waking tolerance of it.

Here are key terms you’ll see throughout NTLA communications and discussions of same.

  • Nex-z (formerly known as NTLA-2001) - This is a gene-editing protocol to address a condition known as ATTR.
  • ATTR (transthyretin amyloidosis) - This is a very serious condition involving the liver. A gene improperly folds. That causes blockages. I’m sure you know, through your own medical situation, that “blockage” is a word you never want a physician to say to you. It’s bad. It can be fatal.
  • ATTR-CM (ATTR with cardiomyopathy) - ATTR accompanied by cardiac-related problems.
  • MAGNITUDE - This is the name of a late-stage Phase III clinical trial involving about 650 persons suffering from ATTR-CM.
  • ATTR-PN (ATTR with polyneuropathy) - ATTR accompanied by nerve-related problems.
  • MAGNITUDE 2 - This is the name of a late-stage Phase III clinical trial involving about 47 participants suffering from ATTR-PN.
  • Ionvo-z (formerly known as NTLA-2002) - This is a gene-editing protocol to address a condition known as HAE.
  • HAE (hereditary angioedema) - This is genetic defect that causes painful swelling in various parts of the body.
  • HAELO - This is the name of a late-stage clinical trial involving about 60 people suffering from HAE.

(2) Our Focus

The relevant issue today concerns testing of Nex-z for patients with ATTR-CM.

This is where participants experienced noteworthy adverse events.

No such events have been reported for patients with ATTR-PN. But as I’ll discuss below, fallout from the ATTR-CM MAGNITUDE study will impact progress in ATTR-PN MAGNITUDE 2 progress.

We are not now concerned with Ionvo-z or HAELO. All there is proceeding on schedule.

All HAELO participants NTLA planned to recruit have by now been enrolled. On the 11/7/25 call, NTLA reported that it is…

on track to share top line data by mid-2026, submit a BLA to the FDA in the second half of 2026, and prepare for an anticipated commercial launch in the U.S. in the first half of 2027.

(3) Chronology— Prelude

I’m going to start by backtracking a bit.

The Street has, for a while, had an antsy love-hate relationship with NTLA.

Back on 10/30/24, I discussed investors having turned against the company based on test data that the Street thought was bad but which the FDA deemed fine.

A bit later, NTLA decided to abandon a project it felt was going nowhere. It chose, instead, to cut costs and confine resources to promising ventures (Nex-Z and Ionvo-z). Considering that pre-commercial biotechs like this should be conserving cash, I thought this was a sensible idea. But as I discussed in my 1/14/25 report, the Street freaked out.

Then, in my 7/18/25 NTLA write-up, I discussed a 5/28/25 adverse event.

An ATTR-CM MAGNITUDE participant was shown to have some abnormally high liver-enzyme tallies. It was asymptomatic — it was only discovered in a routine study-related test.

Had that situation persisted, it would have been dangerous. But that didn’t happen. The situation seems to have resolved on its own without hospitalization or medical action.

The stock fell hard when the issue was first disclosed. But it recovered, and then some, after everything resolved. My 7/18/25 article discussed it further.

NTLA stock thereafter rallied briskly. But let’s not forget the Street’s recent angst. We might assume many recent buyers had, essentially, been dragged kicking and screaming into buying.

So it’s natural to respond to the tragedy at hand by saying, even if not aloud, “Uh oh, here we go again.”

I presented this Prelude to address readers who may understandably think or feel this way. Know, however, that…

Notwithstanding what some Wall Street analysts previously assumed about trial data, Phase I and II test results were good enough for the FDA to allow Nex-z to move on to Phase III. Historically, about 33% of drugs that pass Phases I and II get to move on the Phase III.

Also, note that medically speaking, the latest event has nothing at all to do with the previous adverse event. The latter participant was asymptomatic and his challenging enzme levels resolved spontaneously without hospitalization.

(4) Chronology — The Current Tragedy

On 10/27/25…

NTLA learned that a MAGNITUDE study participant, who had last been dosed on 9/30/24, entered a hospital. That happened after he told his study principal investigator about abdominal pain.

It turned out that two key liver enzymes exceeded the upper limits of normal ranges; one threefold and another fourfold.

Based on the MAGNITUDE protocol, NTLA immediately ceased dosing any patient with Nex-z.

Although not required by the MAGNITUDE-2 protocol, NTLA also chose to cease dosing ATTR-PN patients in that study.

Also, NTLA quickly started…

consulting with clinical investigators and external experts to ensure optimal care for the patient and further assess the event itself. We've now mandated clinical sites to collect additional labs from patients in the initial weeks following dosing to detect potential liver elevation sooner.

Additionally, the company also started consulting with “other potential monitoring and risk mitigation strategies…. (and) global regulatory authorities” to consider other precautionary measures.

Capping it off, CEO John Leonard stated during the 11/7/25 call, that…

we've taken a number of actions in the interest of patient safety. For instance, we've mandated that clinical sites collect additional labs from patients in the initial weeks following dosing to detect potential liver elevation sooner. An internal team has been closely reviewing the blinded safety data, baseline characteristics, among other factors, to identify potential contributors to the liver-related events seen in MAGNITUDE.

On 10/29/25…

The FDA placed formal holds on further MAGNITUDE and MAGNITUDE 2 testing.

On 11/6/25…

The hospitalized participant passed away.

(5) The Cause of Death

With respect to the 80-something participant, Leonard stated on the 11/7/25 call that NTLA had been...

advised by the treating physician that this is a case with complicating comorbidities, and the case is being further evaluated.

As mentioned above, analysts had many follow-up questions. But on the call and to date, Leonard has been and still is unable to provide further answer.

This is frustrating.

But it’s also understandable.

We’re all accustomed to news, fictional and real, in which coroners are able to quickly determine causes of death. Sometimes, though, reality does not cooperate easily with investigators.

The interrelationships between what we know (advanced age, administration of Nex-z, and “complicating comorbidities”) will take time to sort out.

Uncertainty averse as it may be, the Street has no choice but to be patient and await the development of further facts.

Looking Ahead

Besides cause of death, another unknown with which we all must cope is the future of Nex-z.

At this time, Leonard is, absolutely positively not throwing in the towel on this project:

First, ATTR amyloidosis is a disease with high mortality. While undeniable progress has been made in this treatment, current therapies only slow its advance and most patients continue to face progressive morbidity and mortality. Second, we've enrolled more than 650 patients in MAGNITUDE and 47 patients in MAGNITUDE-2. To date, Grade 4 liver transaminase elevations have been reported in less than 1% of all patients enrolled in MAGNITUDE. Each of these cases has been observed approximately 3 to 5 weeks following randomization and dosing. There have been no Grade 4 liver transaminase elevations in MAGNITUDE-2. And third, we are highly encouraged by the data from our ongoing Phase I clinical trial of nex-z. On Monday, in a late-breaker oral session at the 2025 AHA Scientific Sessions in New Orleans, we will have the opportunity to share longer-term data for nexz that we believe demonstrates its potential to improve various disease measures and mortality.

Obviously, though, Leonard is pulling the plug on the timetable for expected progress on Nex-z:

Given the clinical hold, we are unable to maintain our milestone guidance for nex-z, and we expect to provide an update once we finalize the plan with regulators.

As noted above, everything related to Ionvo-z and HAELO is proceeding as previously planned.

But given NTLA’s pre-commercial status and the hold on Nex-Z testing, we have to wonder about financial survivability.

There’s no immediate danger. According to CFO Edward Dulac on the 11/7/25 earnings call:

Our cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities were $669.9 million as of September 30, 2025, compared to $861.7 million as of December 31, 2024. During the third quarter, we raised approximately $115 million from our ATM facility. When combined with the benefits of the restructuring initiatives we implemented in early 2025, this enables us to extend our cash runway into mid-2027 and through lonvo-z's anticipated commercial launch in the U.S. for HAE.

That’s not completely comforting. The currently-uncertain future for Nex-z still makes for a dark cloud.

But Dulac’s remarks suggest NTLA is not now walking along the edge of a cliff on a path covered with banana peels.

Risk

This is obvious The risk involves all things relating to Nex-z.

What to do About NTLA Stock

As a pre-commercial company, valuation ratios have always been inappropriate for NTLA. And now with the ongoing Nex-z crisis, the day when valuation metrics become relevant is being pushed further into the future.

Here’s NTLA’s price chart.

That looks terrible.

The recently strong 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) made an abrupt news-driven U-turn and crashed through the 50-day EMA.

We saw likewise with Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). (Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.)

In my most recent 7/18/25 report, I said “Buy.”

Given the events discussed here, it should come as no surprise that this rating aged horribly. Since then, NTLA is down 30%, versus a 5% S&P 500 gain.

But that’s not the least of it. The stock is now down about 70% from its pre-tragedy peak.

Nobody wants to buy high and sell low. So it would obviously hurt badly to cut and run now. But if one must, then one should.

That said, recognizing our inability to roll the clock back on the post-tragedy pain, and the reality that the current stock price now discounts a lot of bad news, I don’t believe it’s necessary at this time to say “Sell” (which, in my taxonomy, means underperform).

While it is possible that further investigation might reveal an unsolvable problem with Nex-z, the facts now known suggest we need not run to that extreme.

Without making light of a participant death, it’s one person out of the many who’ve received Nex-z. And we have been told that other factors may have contributed to or outright caused the outcome.

Also, we see NTLA working to discover protocols it hopes will pre-identify at-risk persons. That would ultimately put Nex-z on par with many other medications…

Physicians and pharmacists continually stay aware of recognized risk factors and steer at-risk patients away from that which could harm them. (Speaking for myself, GLP-1 drugs could help me a lot, but I’m compelled to avoid them due to high risk, for me, of pancreatic cancer.) So even if we learn that Nex-z is dangerous to some people, we should not assume it will vanish as a failure.

But not saying “Sell” doesn’t mean I can stand pat at a Buy rating (which in my taxonomy, translates to outperform).

Given the MAGNITUDE and MAGNITUDE 2 testing halts, our inability now to guess how long that will last, the worst-case scenario still lurking in the background, and the Street’s habitual angst regarding NTLA (as described above in the chronology Prologue), I find it hard to envision NTLA outperforming the market any time soon.

So for now, and probably for a while, I balance all that by rating NTLA as a neutral relative (to the market) performer. I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by classifying NTLA as a Hold.

data.title: KT Corporation: The Short-Term Headwinds Warrant Holding
data.description:
Introduction And Investment Thesis

I rate KT Corporation (NYSE: KT) as a hold driven by an intention to spend around 1 trillion KRW ($682.417 million) for the US expansion following an alliance with Palantir. While this is a bullish action from the company, this investment has an impact on earnings in FY2026, given that EPS consensus estimates is -28% driven by this huge upfront set to begin in 2026. This is not out of underperformance but from commitment to expand its AI market reach.

This company’s stock price has been bearish since July 2025. I think the reason for this bearish move is the unauthorized micropayments incident, which is under investigation. The company has made plans to compensate for any reported damage pending final investigation, which, in my view, was the driving cause of this bearish move. It is not yet known when the final compensation will be determined.

However, I am rating this company as a hold because following this expansion, there are high expectations in FY2027 when the earnings are expected to record a significant comeback of 29.21% after a projected earnings loss of -28.00 % in 2026. Given the optimism following an alliance with Palantir, I expect a fundamentally grounded bullish reversal in 2027 after a capital expenditure of $682.417 million. This CapEx is set to be utilized in 2026 through 2030 to expand its AI and Cloud technologies in the US, which is a bullish move.

Looking at the AI market CAGR of 38.9% 2025 to 2030, this is a rapid growth. In North America, where it is expanding, it is expected to hold the largest market share, followed by the Asia Pacific. Therefore, given this $1.65 billion upfront investment, this is a significant growth engine, but for now, in my analysis below, I am going deeper to unravel why I think this company is a Hold.

Company Brief

KT Corporation is a telecommunication service provider in South Korea, Asia, and internationally, providing mobile services such as 5G, 4G LTE, 3G W-CDMA, broadband, fixed-line, medical services, data communication, satellite TV, and e-commerce. It provides cloud solutions, handset sales, IT satellites, and financial services, among others. The company was founded in 1981 and is headquartered in Seoul.

My Perspective On Growth Engine

With the understanding that KT offers AI and cloud services, this is the primary reason leading to its alliance with Palantir. Therefore, I will begin by looking at an overall market outlook around AI before getting to the details of my hold perspective.

The AI market is a promising sector, and this can be seen in the rapid growth potential at a CAGR of 38.9% between 2025 and 2030. It is currently valued at $18.22 billion and is expected to reach a market volume of $94.31 billion by 2030.

Most of this growth will be experienced by North America, followed by the Asia Pacific. And here is where the alliance with Palantir makes a strong growth engine for KT. This alliance is an avenue for KT’s entry into the US market. For instance, in KT, combining its cloud and network services with Palantir’s core AI solutions will offer enhanced security to Palantir compared to public clouds. KT Secure Public Cloud will be used to offer Palantir’s system enhanced security and run Palantir’s solutions in a secure environment. Most of Palantir’s customers are in the United States, and therefore, having roots in the US for KT, this positions it for this growth projection mentioned above.

KT is already having a substantial market reach in South Korea with a telephony subscriber market share of 62% and a broadband subscriber market share of 44%. Therefore, if you look at its entry into the US market right, it comes out as a well-thought-out entry, as this is a rapidly growing market after showing substantial reach in its country.

Lately, there have been multiple estimations on Palantir that its market volume may reach $1 trillion by 2030. Given that KT’s alliance with Palantir is a win-win formula, I look at KT to be positioning for this win and will also ride on this growth. Look at it this way, KT will now expand its AX (AI transformation) business in the United States through Palantir to attract companies such as Telecom and Finance to adopt AI. As a primary telecommunication provider, KT will offer the same service to Palantir in South Korea.

Given that KT’s AX business is a primary leverage in this alliance, many businesses cite that among the benefits of AI are innovation, employee satisfaction, and cost, among other benefits; however, adoption is still very low. Only a few organizations are scaling and fully scaled so far, meaning the AI transformation business has a lot of business in enhancing AI adoption.

My Hold outlook

The downside of this optimism is that the stock price has been bearish, and issues such as 368 subscribers facing unauthorized micropayments continue to show a little pessimism. Also, about 22,227 subscribers were affected by an illegal base station connection. Now, the company is planning to replace the total number of USIMs for all its subscribers, and this will cost the company around $139 million for 20 million users.

Adding to the picture that KT is not aware of the amount of compensation that will be awarded to the victims of the incident above, and the damages thereof. I am not convinced of a bullish outlook at the moment because I find a similar headwind for KT with its rival, SK Telecom. SK Telecom was fined $92.739 million by the South Korean Personal Information Protection Committee just recently for negligence and delaying notifying the public of the unauthorized access. According to the framework for arriving at the fines, a company can be fined up to 3% of its sales. For KT, whose FY2024 revenues were 26,431,2 billion KRW, 3% would be a fine of 792,936.12 million KRW ($545.540 million). However, the company can still ask for damage relief to reduce this possible damage.

Financials And Valuation

Over the last 5 years, KT has demonstrated strong revenue growth, which has grown steadily over the years. In Q3’2025, the company recorded a revenue of $7,126.7 billion KRW ($4.907 billion), representing a 7.1% YoY growth. According to consensus revenue estimates, KT’s full-year 2025 revenue is $13.26 billion. Still, its total revenues are trailing at 27,974.8 billion KRW ($19.26 billion), which indicates it has already surpassed the consensus estimate even before Q4’2025, representing a 5.76% increase.

However, the company is entering 2026 facing the above-discussed headwinds, such as compensation and replacing subscribers’ USIM, which may slow revenue collection to about 0.20% increase in 2026.

This uncertainty is also reflected in the EPS because KT is a profitable company and its net income is trailing at 963.586 billion KRW ($663.718 million). This company is already profitable, but the EPS is expected to plummet significantly from 631.20% YoY growth in FY2025 to -28.00% YoY growth in FY2026. KT is expecting to invest most of its earnings into replacing USIM, handling compensation/damages, and expanding its reach in the US through its alliance with Palantir. As it is now, I am expecting a bullish reversal early to late 2026 or early 2027 when these headwinds seem to have been settled satisfactorily.

My Take on Bullish Reversal

Given the optimism surrounding KT corporation discussed above, it has the stamina to sustain its headwinds, as I mentioned above. However, the headwinds currently inform a hold position. Looking at the last 3 months, KT’s price return stands at -10.35% which is significantly below the sector median of -4.42%. This is after a 6-month price return of -6.19% which was also below the sector median of 1.55%.

This weakening price return is an indicator that the market is reacting to the headwinds I have described above. Again, the relative strength also indicates this challenge as the RSI has hovered around 55 and is currently below 50 and phasing downwards, which suggests an ongoing bearish momentum. I therefore expect a grounded performance towards the end of 2026 to early 2027, when the company will start showing signs of relief from these hiccups.

Investment Risks

Regulatory Risk From Cybersecurity Breach

The existing unresolved scrutiny over the concealment of a primary malware infection across its servers is a significant ground for regulatory risk. The KT faces fines and operational restrictions from the Personal Information Protection Committee. This regulatory overhang may also delay KY dividend recovery, which then risks outweighing the likelihood of early 2026 recovery. The company is also facing reputational damage following this matter, which could lead to customer attrition in sensitive sectors such as healthcare and finance.

Execution risk in US expansion

KT’s entry into the US via Palantir’s ecosystem as its first major international AI push is an opportunity, but it also has a fair share of risks associated with it. This entry involves unfamiliar US regulatory uncertainties, competitive pressures, and possible high onboarding costs. For example, the company plans to divide its onboard entry costs into phases, starting from 2026 to 2030. If the company encounters challenges in monetizing foundry and Gotham deployments for Cloud AI, this would delay ROI and extend the margin pressure into 2027

Conclusion

While KT shows potential from the large market share in South Korea and expansion to the US, the company is facing headwinds that blur its future outlook, particularly in 2026. Replacement of USIM and damages from cybersecurity leaks are some of the reasons, I reiterate my hold stance.

data.title: International Paper: All Eyes On The Balance Sheet
data.description:
Summary

International Paper (IP) is facing significant headwinds in its European business, which is in the process of being integrated following the $9.9 billion all-stock acquisition of DS Smith that closed at the end of January 2025.

In the United States, box shipment volumes have been much weaker than initially expected so far in 2025, and that puts additional pressure on free cash flows. With $9.0 billion of net debt on the balance sheet and $1.85 billion in capital expenditures expected for 2025, the pressure is slowly increasing on the company's balance sheet. Without some sort of recovery in corrugated cardboard demand in the next few quarters, more and more questions will probably arise about the company's capital structure. At least for now, I do not have the necessary conviction to dip my toes and buy some shares.

From Timberlands To Corrugated Cardboard

With my ongoing research about the forest industry, I approached International Paper with the idea of finding a large pulp and paper producer, as the name seemed to imply. However, as discussed in the third quarter conference call, International Paper is in the midst of a large transformation and is now a pure-play packaging company, following the $1.5 billion divestment of its Global Cellulose Fibres business to the private equity firm American Industrial Partners. In that sense, I wonder if International Paper still belongs within the iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (WOOD), an exchange-traded fund that I plan to cover in the next few days.

Post the Global Cellulose Fibres sale and the exit of some specialty businesses and low margin export, we will be exclusively a sustainable packaging business. This is a major milestone in our transformation.

As illustrated below, the company now divides its operations into two packaging solutions segments and derives the vast majority of its revenues from corrugated packaging and containerboard. To add some details about the terms used in the industry, corrugated cardboard consists of three layers: the two flat outer layers, called linerboard, and the corrugating flute in the middle. In this manufacturing process, containerboard is the starting material while corrugated packages are the end product. As the largest producer in North America, International Paper is likely behind some of the Amazon boxes you have recently ordered. Other important players in the corrugated packaging business include Smurfit Westrock (SW), Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) and Greif (GEF), among others. This opens up a completely new area of research that I don't know much about, which is the global container and packaging industry. The petrochemical business is probably not far away either, if we talk about plastic packaging. One day, I will spend some time studying this important segment of the economy; I just don't know when.

Over the last two weeks or so, I have published my research about four publicly listed timberland owners in North America. In descending size order, the largest one is Weyerhaeuser (WY) with a timberland portfolio covering 10.4 million acres. This is followed by PotlachDeltic (PCH) and Rayonier (RYN) with their 2.1 million and 2.0 million acres of timberlands, respectively. Finally, Acadian Timber (ADN:CA) owns 1.1 million acres of timberland primarily located in the province of New Brunswick in Canada. The reason why I mention all of those details is that those companies form the very foundation of the global supply chain that International Paper operates in.

If we go one step ahead in the supply chain, we find lumber producers like West Fraser (WFG) and Interfor (IFP:CA), among many others. If we add another layer of transformation, we find oriented stain board and plywood manufacturers like Boise Cascade (BCC). The exterior wood sidings produced by Louisiana-Pacific (LPX) would be another step ahead in the supply chain, given their differentiation and specialty uses in some cases. LPX is the only company I have researched so far in the industry with some sort of pricing power. Other than the differentiated and branded wood sidings sold by Louisiana-Pacific, we are dealing with heavily commoditized products.

To arrive at International Paper and the corrugated cardboard business, we only have to follow what happens to raw sawlogs when they enter a sawmill. The bark, wood chips and sawdust produced as by-products of the milling process form the raw material to produce pulp, which is in turn used to produce paper and cardboard.

Researching International Paper made me realize how trees are everywhere in our daily lives. As many of you know, I have been living in a yurt in the boreal forest of Northern Québec for more than three years now. Resolute Forest Products, now owned by the Paper Excellence Group, came to my area last year to harvest white spruce. I am now in a much better position to understand what happens to those trees following the very first step of harvesting.

DS Smith Acquisition

In April 2024, International Paper announced an all-stock business combination with DS Smith, the second-largest producer of containerboard in Europe, with a market share estimated at ~10% as of 2020. After the divestment of some assets in France, Portugal and Spain to comply with the antitrust ruling of the European Commission, the transaction closed at the end of January 2025. International Paper then issued to DS Smith's shareholders 178.1 million newly created shares worth $9.9 billion, which is approximately half the current market capitalization of $19.7 billion.

As illustrated below, International Paper now operates a sizeable manufacturing base in Europe, with a portfolio of ~250 mills and plants all throughout the continent. It is important to understand that in the packaging business, proximity to the end customer is a key competitive advantage, as it rapidly becomes uneconomical to ship containerboard over long trucking distances. This reminds me of my research about lumber mills and the cost advantages of being as close as possible to productive timberlands. To be a low-cost producer, location is key in both the containerboard and lumber businesses. Other than that, we are pretty much dealing with pure commodities with long cycles following supply and demand fundamentals.

Talking about the cyclicality of the corrugated containerboard market, the European market is currently in the midst of a severe downturn where operating conditions are particularly difficult. As discussed by IP's management during the Baird Annual Global Industrial Conference, the profitability of the European business has been under significant pressure, and the segment is currently under aggressive restructuring. The North American market also presents its own challenges, but the real weakness is in Europe. This obviously raises some questions about the April 2024 transaction announcement with DS Smith. With some much going on in the European segment, we will probably have to wait for a few more quarters before seeing the real cash flow generation capabilities of the newly acquired assets.

Unlike what has happened in North America, which has a much more solid supply-demand dynamic in Europe that doesn't, we have seen pricing drop there also. And so the total is almost $300 million of year-over-year impact to profit in Europe. So we are aggressively administering the same playbook. It comes in a little bit different flavor in Europe because of the structure in Europe, but we are very aggressively restructuring Europe.

North American Packaging Solutions

As discussed in the third quarter conference call, International Paper started 2025 with the expectation of U.S. box shipments to grow between +1.0% and +1.5%. However, management revised those estimates to a decline between -1.0% and -1.5%, which is the exact opposite of what was initially expected. It is also important to highlight how International Paper underperformed the market in terms of volume trend. On a more positive note, the chart below shows a significant turnaround since the end of 2024. This roughly coincides with the appointment of IP's new CEO, Andrew Silvernail, who took office on May 1st, 2025 and the implementation of a more customer-centric approach.

Given the challenging market conditions in the United States, International Paper announced last week the closure of the packaging plants in California and Kentucky. This comes after the August 2025 closure announcement of two facilities in Georgia. On top, we only have to go back to February 2025 to find the closure announcement of four additional mills and plants in the United States.

The trend is clear: International Paper is rationalizing its asset base in both Europe and the United States. Market conditions are particularly difficult in Europe, but it is not all colours and rainbows in the United States either. Market developments in the United States will continue to be the main driver of IP's overall financial performance, as the segment accounted for ~75% of consolidated EBITDA. In the meantime, IP is also in the process of integrating the recently acquired DS Smith's operations, a process full of uncertainty when it comes to corporate or restructuring expenses.

Balance Sheet & Valuation

International Paper ended the third quarter with $995 million in cash and total debt of $10.0 billion. Based on the current $19.7 billion market capitalization, IP's net debt of $9.0 billion represents a leverage ratio slightly above 30%. With an undrawn $1.0 billion commercial paper program and $1.9 billion available on its credit facilities, the company is in a relatively good liquidity position at the moment. Given its scale and investment-grade credit rating at Moody's and S&P, International Paper is among the companies with the best access to capital relative to some of its more distant peers in the forest industry.

Operating within the North American and European corrugated packaging business remains a capital-intensive endeavour, as reflected by IP's 2025 capital expenditure guidance of ~$1.85 billion. If market conditions in the United States were to deteriorate further, it would quickly be felt on the company's cash flow generation, and this is when IP's liquidity position could start to deteriorate. According to the most recent earnings call, management is not expecting a recovery in Europe anytime soon, so the question becomes whether the U.S. business will be able to keep International Paper afloat during the ongoing storm.

The bottom 25% of paper producers in Europe are in a very, very tough position right now, right? On a cash basis, they are in a very tough position. I am not foolish enough to believe that there is going to be some kind of mass decision making to take out capacity. You see a lot of resilience in terms of holding on by privately held long-term family companies. So, I am not looking for a miracle there, and we should not. We should be executing as we can. We would expect, however, modest price improvement over that timeframe.

Under the long-term guidance found in the Q3-25 earnings presentation, International Paper expects to generate free cash flows of approximately $2.0 billion in 2027. It would translate into a forward free cash flow yield of ~10% relative to IP's current $19.7 billion market capitalization. Having said that, this long-term forecast comes with quite a bit of risk, starting with DS Smith's integration and the dynamic market conditions in both the United States and Europe. If the economic environment continues to deteriorate, International Paper may find itself burning cash instead of generating free cash flows. That would be, once again, the exact opposite of what was initially planned.

Conclusion

International Paper is the largest manufacturer of corrugated containerboard in North America. Contrary to what the company's name seems to imply, IP is now a pure-play packaging company since the divestment of its Global Cellulose Fibres business.

The company's most important challenges are currently in Europe, as the integration of the DS Smith acquisition is still ongoing and must be done in the midst of challenging market conditions. In both Europe and North America, International Paper is in the middle of a significant reorganization process, as evidenced by the numerous asset closures announced so far this year.

Assuming everything goes according to management's projections over the next two years or so, International Paper currently trades at a forward free cash flow yield of ~10%. My 'Hold' rating reflects my personal view that this is grossly insufficient to compensate for all the market and operational risks involved. If market conditions stay where they are in Europe and continue to deteriorate in the United States, International Paper may find itself burning cash instead of creating free cash flows. This is when the balance sheet and the company's liquidity profile would come into focus.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: For StubHub, It's About Trust, Not The Numbers
data.description:
On its face, the stunning plunge in StubHub (STUB) stock since its September initial public offering seems like it could be an opportunity. The company's first earnings report was ugly, but on its own doesn't seem to support a 36% plunge over the last three sessions. Trading in the space has been ugly, but Live Nation (LYV) is facing political pressure and Vivid Seats (SEAT) is struggling at least in part because it is hemorrhaging market share to StubHub itself.

More broadly, the mid-term story for StubHub that led to an IPO at a ~$10 billion valuation hardly seems broken. There are material, external factors at play at the moment pressuring the company's results. StubHub is also investing behind the business in several key initiatives; as those efforts turn from increasing costs to driving revenue, margins and profits should inflect higher.

Fundamentally, it is quite easy to make a bull case for StubHub stock. The argument for owning the StubHub business, however, is a bit more difficult.

STUB Stock Gets Cheap

StubHub went public at a rather high valuation: in the range of 35x this year's Adjusted EBITDA estimates. With the stock down nearly half from the IPO price, valuation looks much more constructive.

Trailing twelve-month Adjusted EBITDA is $274 million, putting EV/EBITDA right around 20x. That still seems high, but in context looks like it should be an absolute steal. On the Q3 call, chief financial officer Connie James noted that excluding cash interest and outflows related to the timing of Taylor Swift's monster Eras tour last year, trailing twelve-month free cash flow is slightly higher, at $279 million. Cash interest is coming down to ~$100 million as IPO proceeds went to pay down debt; that suggests normalized free cash flow of about $180 million, putting price to free cash flow at about 25x. (It's not entirely clear why management is pulling out cash interest from a measure of "free cash flow" discussed on the call; I'm assuming they are referring to unlevered free cash flow but in this piece I am using the levered number for valuation.)

To be sure, that multiple doesn't necessarily scream value, either. But EBITDA is likely to jump substantially going forward. StubHub of late has been heavily investing in the business to take share and build out new revenue streams. An effort to build out direct issuance capabilities — in which venues can sell directly on the platform — has shown some early success, but clearly a lot of expense. (The effort even has a newly hired vice president.) Take rate, the percentage of the transaction kept by StubHub, has come down as the company looks to outperform smaller rivals on pricing. Federally mandated all-in pricing has provided a headwind this year — management believes it has compressed the market by about 10%, and Vivid Seats has cited state-level experience that supports that analysis — but the headwind will be lapped during Q2 2026.

At the moment, sell-side consensus for 2026 EBITDA is $870 million — more than triple the TTM figure. No doubt, those estimates are coming down as we speak, but the broader point is that StubHub's profits and cash flow should be near a multi-year bottom, and have a clear path toward inflecting in 2026. Indeed, while the lack of guidance for Q4 clearly spooked the market (particularly given that management also admitted that some events had slipped into Q3, casting a bit of shade on a top-line beat), chief executive officer Eric Baker insisted repeatedly on the call that the company was set up for a strong 2026.

Even if a 'strong' 2026 is not where Street analysts projected, there's still a path for STUB stock to look absurdly cheap here. $600 million in EBITDA suggests EV/EBITDA under 10x and P/FCF, even normalized for taxes (StubHub has over $1 billion in net operating loss carryforwards that would lower its current cash tax rate) likely around 10x. For a clear leader in the secondary market with solid EBITDA margins, huge cash conversion, and excellent return on invested capital, that multiple seems too low.

Short- And Long-Term Returns

And so there is a case that by overreacting, and overreacting badly, to the Q3 release, the market has created an exceptionally attractive opportunity to buy the dip. Even with 2026 expectations coming down, on paper there's still room for a clear double back to the range of the IPO price. Mid-teens EV/EBITDA and ~20x free cash flow both are reasonable multiples. It is disappointing that StubHub didn't give guidance for Q4 (particularly since the release came two-plus weeks into the quarter), but a single quarter shouldn't break the case here. If the company can re-establish credibility with solid performance in 2026, STUB stock can pretty easily double.

The longer-term upside is much higher. Again, investments are going to start paying off next year, but STUB is not just a 12-month play. The direct issuance market is enormous: management has cited an addressable market of about $100 billion, and it does make sense that venues, sports teams (StubHub has developed a partnership with Major League Baseball for direct ticketing), and other entities would want to find a way to monetize potentially unused inventory. (Importantly, they can do so under someone else's brand, rather than diluting their own product; buyers ostensibly would not know from where the tickets were sourced.)

Baker also clearly is looking to consolidate share in the legacy secondary market. Perhaps lost in the analysis of the quarter was a notable claim from Baker on the call: since 2022, StubHub has gone from being about the same size in North America as its largest competitor (this would appear to be SeatGeek) to being four times as large. SeatGeek is private, but there is potential support for Baker's claim there: after terminating a SPAC (special purpose acquisition company) merger in 2022, SeatGeek reportedly has been eyeing an IPO of its own, but has failed to do so. We know StubHub is taking share from Vivid: the latter company's guidance suggests its GOV (gross order value, or the total value of transactions conducted on the platform) will be down about 40% this year versus 2024.

Advertising, a high-margin revenue stream that has boosted profitability at other platform businesses, is on the way. StubHub is starting small, with sponsored listings and corporate partnerships (including one with Booking Holdings (BKNG)), but there is a clear path toward tens of millions of dollars in revenue at incremental margins that should be quite high. That's material against the current normalized FCF base below $300 million.

The reason StubHub was able to price the IPO at $23.50 is because investors at the time understood that 2025 was going to be an investment year and because there were multiple long-term growth drivers for the business across direct issuance, advertising, and share gains. Taking the long view, those drivers all remain intact; Q3 results, and the lack of guidance for Q4, doesn't change that fact. Sell-side analysts immediately after an IPO always are taken with a grain of salt, but less than six weeks Goldman Sachs put out a $46 price target on STUB stock, nearly quadruple Tuesday's close. It's difficult to see Q3 results (in which revenue rose 11% year-over-year) as evidence the target was that wrong. Here, too, the case for triple-digit upside seems at worst enticing.

Where Is This All Going?

The one concern, however, is whether the strategy is going to work. The political atmosphere is one obvious risk. Two days after the StubHub IPO, the Federal Trade Commission sued Live Nation and Ticketmaster for illegal tactics in the resale market. The United Kingdom this week banned the resale of tickets above face value; though that doesn't affect StubHub (it divested its U.K. business as a condition of its merger with Viagogo), it's another sign of broader discontent among customers and regulators with the ticket business.

On the Q3 call, an analyst asked about so-called "speculative sellers", who basically sell tickets they don't own ahead of an event (the idea being they can eventually buy similar or better seats for a lower price; the practice is often compared to short selling a stock). Baker's answer was frankly dismissive, even though the practice continues to grow and the FTC has said publicly that it may violate existing rules.

There are worries as to whether direct issuance really is going to be a big market, given that it may simply wind up taking revenue away from the primary market, leaving venues and operators in essentially the same place (or worse, if consumers get used to the idea that a flood of cheap tickets will arrive not long before the event). Market share gains are impressive, but as I noted just ahead of the IPO it's not as if StubHub's long-term growth profile is not that impressive. There's a clear concern that the lower take rate from Q3 is a sign of things to come, and that in turn suggests lower-than-projected EBITDA margins going forward even if those margins improve in 2026 and beyond.

In that context, the sell-off following Q3 perhaps makes more sense than the numbers might suggest. StubHub's strategy under Baker clearly is to become a legitimate rival to Ticketmaster, not just the leader in the secondary market. But given political crosswinds and industry changes, that requires exceptionally nimble management. Venues and teams continue to try and capture more value in the primary market (variable pricing for single-game tickets, etc.). Regulators at the state and federal level are eyeing the profits being made in the secondary market. Consumers are unhappy. StubHub's long-term strategy is full of potential, but it will also require a management team that can navigate a highly uncertain environment. Instead of inspiring confidence on that front in the company's first earnings report, management instead provided more uncertainty, with the lack of guidance as well as somewhat circumspect language suggesting a soft Q4.

STUB remains a story that absolutely can work. Again, there are paths to 100%-plus upside by next year and a triple or better over a multi-year timeframe. (This is still a platform business, so incremental margins are high, particularly because Baker did say after Q3 that direct issuance take rates are roughly the same as those in the secondary market.) And even with the concerns, I'll admit to being tempted to trying to time the bottom, and betting that the market is overreacting here.

But at the same time, it is a platform that (as I detailed in the first article) has suffered since the Viagogo merger, and with a management team that pretty much tanked its first-ever earnings report. It may just have been a one-time slip-up, but the key risk to StubHub stock is that Q3 is actually a sign of things to come.

data.title: Rimini Street: Q3 Missed Optically, But The Core Business Is Still Intact
data.description:
Back in August, I called Rimini Street, Inc. (RMNI) a Buy primarily because the company was getting out of the woods from the legal overhang with Oracle, and the underlying growth was good. It's 3 months later, new quarterly results are in, and the stock has dipped ~11% in the intervening period.

That selloff doesn't seem justified if we consider Q3's results, and the reasons for it don't line up with what I believe to be the most important things to consider long term. Yes, the reported revenue is soft, and non-GAAP profitability took a small hit, and I can understand why the sentiment turned. But I don't believe that the market is looking at the crucial parts of the business: Remaining Performance Obligations (RPOs) are up to a record figure, as are retention and billings. The business is also actively returning capital to shareholders and has a clean balance sheet.

All these things are what I built August's position on, and they haven't deteriorated. Now that the stock is priced even cheaper than then, I believe that the margin of safety is even wider, and I'm staying on the Buy side.

A Snapshot of RMNI's Q3 2025

I have laid out the most important things from Rimini Street's Q3 2025 results in the table above. I want to discuss why the company's performance supports a more bullish perspective, not the slight dip we've had since the end of October.

First, the revenue decline is barely noticeable, at 1% YoY, but it is vital that we remember the reason for it: RMNI is winding down its legacy PeopleSoft support. And we can see that the business itself is in pretty good shape, as indicated by its Adjusted Calculated Billings growing 6.7% compared to the same period last year. RPOs are also up to $611 million, a 9.3% increase when you take out PeopleSoft-related backlog.

So, we have a higher backlog, a good renewal base, and the company's active client count is up to 3,155. None of these indicators fit the narrative that a slight revenue and EPS miss seems to be driving.

Further on from that, let us look at the margins. Gross margin is still around 60% from both a GAAP and non-GAAP perspective, so it's clear that there's nothing wrong on an operational level. I must point out that operating income and adjusted EBITDA, both non-GAAP, dipped considerably compared to last year's numbers, but that's likely a consequence of the timing around service delivery, along with higher operating expense absorption.

In my opinion, these are unlikely to continue in Q4, and even though the adjusted profitability metrics are softer, they don't affect the foundation of my Buy position. It just means that now, I may have to adjust my EBITDA expectations for the full fiscal year.

Finally, we have the balance sheet, which is in much better shape than in Q2. Cash sits at ~$109 million, and the company has paid off its remaining revolver. That leaves only ~$70 million in long-term debt, so RMNI is basically in a net cash position now. Remember that this is a company that was trading like it might need to raise more capital just a few years ago, when it was waist-deep in litigation.

All of that is in the background now, and RMNI is using this newfound freedom to increase value for its shareholders. The company spent $3.8 million in Q3 on buying back around 900,000 shares, and I like that it has extended the repurchase authorization through 2029. There's well over $40 million left that can be spent on buybacks, and it's a very useful lever for management, given today's cheap multiples on its stock.

My Position Is Unchanged

In addition to everything that I outlined above, I'm very confident that RMNI will remain a strong performer because of its fundamentals. It's doing well with the transition into managed services, third-party cloud support, and AI-enabled ERP advisory, and there's nothing in Q3's numbers that contradicts that.

The only thing we really need to watch out for, at least in the short term, is profitability. I mentioned the dip in earnings earlier, and it means that the ramp I was expecting will be slower. As such, I'm also willing to adjust my reasoning, and the fix is straightforward. Let us assume that instead of full-year EBITDA snapping back toward the low 50s, it only comes out to ~$45 million for FY25. Then we wait for the cost base to reset, and the PeopleSoft runoff loses its impact.

That's the tighter setup I'm going for, and the rest of my thesis still holds. Now, let us look at the valuation today.

RMNI Is Still Cheap For What It Is

In my view, RMNI is still trading too cheaply for what it is, and today's valuation is still in investors' favor. Again, this is a business in a net cash position of ~$38 million, going off the debt and cash figures in the Q3 report. Its market cap is $353 million, and its enterprise value is $337 million, per Seeking Alpha's calculations.

So, the stock is trading at 6-7x EV/EBITDA if we use a more deliberate FY25 target of ~$45 million in EBITDA. The table I compiled in the earlier section already shows us why the multiple looks out of line because the fundamentals don't support the idea that the business deserves such a distressed multiple. They show, quite clearly, that RMNI's business model justifies trading closer to 9 or 10x EBITDA in a normal environment.

Here's how I see the upside, going with some realistic assumptions.

  • Base Case (8.5x EBITDA on $47 million)

Here, I'm assuming that the market closes the valuation gap as the company's profitability is more stable. We get an EV of ~$400 million, and the fair value of the stock comes out to ~$4.8/share.

  • Bull Case (9.5x EBITDA on $50 million)

I'm assuming that RMNI achieves better operating leverage as the runoff from legacy PeopleSoft fades and managed services continue to expand.

Fair value: ~$5.6/share

  • Bear Case (7x EBITDA on $45 million)

Basically, the market is pricing in one or two more quarters of margin pressure.

Fair value: ~$3.85/share

That's not far off today's price, so the spread here is very favorable. I say the downside is already covered by the company's net cash position, steady gross margins, and sticky recurring revenue. What we need to see here is a modest uptick in the multiples.

Risks

I've already touched on the decline in adjusted profitability metrics and the drag from legacy runoffs, so I'll discuss other risks here. First, execution risk is still a consideration. The upside I've described largely depends on RMNI becoming more than just a pure maintenance business, and we're seeing very promising signs of a successful transition. However, it depends on consistent delivery, staffing, and client expansion, so the company needs to keep delivering on all those fronts.

Besides those, we must also remember that macrosensitivity is a key factor in RMNI's industry. A lot of the company's new offerings are tied to modernization projects, and if clients delay their decisions into later quarters, it affects the topline and margins. Fortunately, the company has a pretty hefty backlog, so I'm not too worried about this either.

Your Takeaway

Nothing in Q3's results changes my arguments for RMNI, and the stock sold off because the quarter missed a few targets. That's all, and it doesn't change my long-term outlook. The multiples are still cheap, and the cash position and buybacks give the stock a solid floor. Consequently, I'm reiterating my Buy recommendation.

data.title: Elbit Systems: Backlog Supports Growth, But Valuation And Cash Flows Limit Upside
data.description:
Elbit Systems (ESLT) stock price has substantially increased in value since my buy rating in August 2024 surging 155% against the S&P 500’s 18.8% gain making it one of the aerospace and defense top performers. However, in September I downgraded the stock from buy to hold and we see that the stock gained just 0.5% against the 1.2% gain for the S&P 500. In this report, I discuss why Elbit System has been underperforming, analyze the Q3 2025 earnings and I update my stock price target and explain why I believe the stock remains a hold.

Growth Narrative Shifts From Israel To International Defense Sales

If we look beyond the performance since September, we can only conclude that the stock price performance has been impressive. The major outperformance against the market performance eventually can also lead to underperformance. However, for Elbit there is also a shift in the growth dynamics. Demand for weapon systems remains elevated globally. However, previously Elbit Systems benefited from increased weapon system sales to Israel in support of the war in Gaza and with a ceasefire in place and some relative stability in the region the growth prospects for sales to the IDF are fading and shifting more towards international sales.

Yaacov Kagan, CFO of Elbit Systems, said the following about that during the Q3 2025 earnings call:

We are targeting around flattish backlog in Israel and growth outside of Israel, predominantly in Europe. That will be the growth area, which -- we see our funnel, we see our opportunities, and we see the demand that's coming out from Europe. And we think that this is the place that predominantly will provide the growth in the future in the backlog.

That is not to say that sales to Israel are going to implode or decline necessarily, but there may be some normalization in the share of sales to Israel. For the first nine months of the year, the share of sales to Israel was 33.2% compared to 28.5% in the first nine months of 2024 and 29.1% for the full year. With the growth shifting, we are just going to see more sales going to come from European demand fulfilment.

Elbit Systems Sales And Margins Grow

During the third quarter, sales grew 12% to $1.9 billion. Aerospace sales declined modestly by 2.9% to $477.7 million. C4I and Cyber sales grew 14% to $243.3 million, ISTAR and EW which includes Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance and Electronic Warfare grew 5.2% to $593.7 million, Land Systems sales grew 40.8% to $593.7 million, and Elbit Systems of America sales declined 2.1% to $407.7 million. In Aerospace, sales declined due to lower precision guided munition sales while C4I and Cyber sales grew due to radio systems and C2 (Command & Control) sales in Europe. ISTAR and EW sales growth was driven by higher electro-optic and electronic warfare sales in Israel while Land revenues were driven by munition demand from Europe and Israel. Sales in the US decreased due to lower electronic system sales partially offset by higher demand for maritime and warfighter system sales.

Operating income grew 33% to $187 million indicating margins of 9.7% compared to 8.2% a year ago. Operating cash flow increased by $100 million to $157 million while free cash flow increased by $96 million to $101 million and backlog grew 14% to $25.2 billion. So, we do see margin improvement, better cash conversion and significant backlog.

Order Backlog Supports 3.3x Annual Sales

While the backlog composition is expected to start leaning increasingly on international sales and primarily sales to Europe, we note that the backlog growth indicating substantial upside to revenues. Backlog has grown 18% annually since 2020 while revenues grew “only” 11% and the current backlog of $25.2 billion covers 3.27x twelve-month trailing sales. Typically, I believe a healthy ballpark figure is a 2.5 to 3 year backlog coverage. For Elbit Systems, this would indicate $8.4 billion in annual sales at minimum and that would indicate support for around 9% sales growth. A 2.5 year coverage would indicate revenue potential north of $10 billion but that is something that I do not expect to happen until 2028.

Elbit Systems Stock Remains A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Elbit Systems, I am increasing my price target from $520.53 to $530.80. The increase is driven by higher expected EBITDA. Between 2025 and 2027, EBITDA estimates have increased 2.7% with a 0.8% downward revision in 2025%, up 2.5% in 2026 and up 5.5% in 2027. The CAGR has improved from 19% to 21.6%. Free cash flow estimates have decreased by 3.8% this year, up modestly for 2026 and down by 8.6% in 2027 as capital expenditures increase to facilitate sales growth in Europe. The total downward revision is 4.2% with a CAGR of 16.9%, down from 20.7% expected earlier. Given the single digit upside, the hold rating for Elbit Systems remains.

While I believe that the stock remains a hold and is not yet a buy, the underlying metrics are expected to trend well with continued margin growth as operating leverage improves further. One metric that does not impress at the moment is the cash flow. We see that operating cash flow growth is expected to flatten. So, the downward revision on free cash flow is not solely driven by higher capital expenditures. I believe the flattening in the operating cash flow growth in 2027 is primarily driven by the tapering of growth in cash flows from sales to the IDF. For 2026, the company is expected to enter a net cash position, which does open up room for dividend increases and inorganic growth opportunities.

Conclusion: Elbit Systems Stock Is Growth At A High Price

I believe that Elbit Systems is not necessarily unattractive, but I am not a big fan of the prospects of tapering operating cash flow growth and the current valuation. It results in relatively low upside for next year which also provides support to maintain my hold rating. Europe is likely going to be a growth area for Elbit Systems and the company will also invest to grow its presence in Europe, but it is not enough to warrant a buy rating.

data.title: Hints Of A Weak Jobs Market Ahead Of Thursday's Payrolls Report
data.description:
The government has reopened, and the task of piecing together the missing economic data points has started. Early clues suggest that hiring slowed in October. Meanwhile, the Labor Department will release its delayed payrolls report for September tomorrow.

Reviewing a bit of new continuing claims data from the Labor Department, Reuters reports: “The number of Americans on jobless benefits surged between mid-September and mid-October, government data showed on Tuesday, suggesting an elevated unemployment rate in October as an uncertain economic environment discourages hiring.”

Fed Governor Chris Waller weighed in this week and cited “eye-popping” layoffs from major companies and a slump in consumer confidence. He added:

“I am not worried about inflation accelerating or inflation expectations rising significantly. My focus is on the labor market, and after months of weakening, it is unlikely that the September jobs report later this week or any other data in the next few weeks would change my view that another cut is in order.”

The view of economists for Thursday’s release of payrolls data for September indicates that hiring picked up modestly, albeit from a sluggish level. Econoday.com’s consensus point forecast calls for a 50,000 increase in non-farm payrolls. That’s an improvement from August’s weak 22,000 increase, but not by much. If correct, the September update will align with signs of a slowing labor market in October via other sources.

ADP’s weekly employment report, for instance, shows that US companies cut 2,500 jobs per week on average in the four weeks through Nov. 1.

Revelio Labs says payrolls for October fell 9,100, according to its estimate.

Despite signs of a soft labor market, market expectations have turned cautious on the outlook for another rate cut by the Federal Reserve at next month’s policy meeting. The recent confidence that the central bank would cut for a third time on Dec. 10 has faded to a coin flip for estimating the odds, based on Fed funds futures.

Although the Fed’s Waller has been leaning in favor of additional cuts, disagreement among Fed officials has widened lately as concerns about inflation persist.

“It’s reflective of a ton of uncertainty,” said Luke Tilley, chief economist at M&T Bank. “It’s not surprising at all that there’s a wide divergence of opinions.”

Fed Chairman Powell signaled the fuzzy outlook for policy recently, noting: “A further reduction in the policy rate at the December meeting is not a foregone conclusion—far from it,” he said late last month. “Policy is not on a preset course.”

Markets will be keenly focused on Thursday’s payrolls report for deciding if Powell’s guidance is due for an update.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NRG Energy: Buy Into A Structurally Tight Power Market And Data Center Super-Cycle
data.description:
Thesis

A company that is in the middle of a structural shift in the U.S. electricity market is NRG Energy (NYSE:NRG).

The demand is rising strongly due to electrification, the return of industrial production and the immense growth of data centers.

In this context, the company combines strong profitability, disciplined capital allocation and a clear investment strategy for growth.

Q32025 results were excellent and justify the (for the third consecutive year) guidance upgrade, with EBITDA and EPS at historically high levels.

At the same time the company is building a comprehensive portfolio of data center power, with 445 MW already contracted. The pipeline reaches 5.4 GW through the collaboration with GE Vernova and Kiewit, with prices now above $80/MWh.

The acquisition of LS Power, which is currently being finalized, critically strengthens the production base and geographic diversification.

With a CAGR of approximately 14% EPS through 2029, with no data center contribution yet, the stock offers a rare combination of visible growth, strong cash flow, and aggressive capital returns.

I've written about NRG before, and in this environment and I remain with a "BUY" recommendation.

Corporate Profile and Strategic Landscape

As I see it, NRG has evolved from a traditional producer to an integrated energy and services platform for the end consumer.

The company operates in key competitive markets with a focus on ERCOT, PJM and select East Coast states.

Electricity demand in Texas has grown nearly 30% in five years, according to administration data.

This growth is being fueled by population dynamics, electrification, industrial relocation and energy-intensive artificial intelligence applications.

Through the acquisition of LS Power and the Texas Energy Fund program, it is adding approximately 15 GW of natural gas and 7 GW of virtual power plants.

At the same time, it is developing partnerships targeting an additional 6 GW of capacity under joint development with strategic partners.

Smart Home is also a very important pillar as NRG controls one of the largest portfolios of connected homes.

Growth is strong, with double-digit profit growth and very high customer loyalty.

The home-based virtual power plant gives NRG the ability to pool flexible resources from thousands of homes.

This enhances its value in the power mechanism, reduces exposure to extreme events and improves the customer experience.

This demonstrates a sense of balance between growth and shareholder returns.

The capital return program covers buybacks of at least $1.3 billion in 2025 and a fresh authorization of up to $3 billion through 2028.

Meanwhile, aiming for 7%-9% annual dividend growth per share generates a predictable, stable stream of returns.

The company is, in a general sense, the rare look of something growing fast and still being capital disciplined.

Data Centers, BYOP and the LS Power Transformation

The main catalyst for the next five years is NRG’s role in powering the next generation of data centers.

For reasons of cost and reliability, the weight is moving toward the “Bring Your Own Power” model that a growing number of major tech companies have settled on.

And instead of depending on regulated tariffs they incur the full costs of new generation capacity to serve their own loads.

NRG is in privileged position, with available sites, commercial expertise and access to large-scale capital.

So far, 445 MW of the long-term agreements have been executed in ERCOT and PJM out to 2032. Prices have hit above $80 a MWh already, more than the administration’s earlier target range.

The pipeline of agreements under Letters of Intent and joint development with GE Vernova and Kiewit reaches 5.4 GW.

This picture shows an escalation of interest from hyperscalers and data center developers in an environment of limited new capacity.

The acquisition of LS Power complements this strategy.

The company is becoming a big, independent power producer in America with exposure to long term demand expansion.

If part of the 5.4 GW pipeline is locked in on terms similar to the existing deals, the upside margin becomes very significant.

The market, for now, seems to be conservatively pricing both LS Power’s potential and the future value of AI contracts.

Financials

Q32025 financial results show that NRG is executing strongly in every area.

Adjusted EBITDA reached historic high and adjusted EPS increased by 32% y-o-y.

In the nine month period EPS improved by 36% and Cash flow before growth investments rose than last year’s levels due to stronger profitability and working capital.

Stand-alone guidance for 2026 shows further EBITDA growth, mainly from higher prices in Texas and previous acquisitions.

Higher financial expenses and taxes are slightly limiting free cash flow growth but not stopping it.

Even without LS Power, the profitability profile remains strong, while the acquisition clearly adds additional potential.

Valuation & Forward Scenarios for 2026

NRG currently trades at around 19x earnings, a level that reflects how the market continues to view the company as a mature energy producer.

But the picture has now changed. The rapid expansion of BYOP deals, the growing momentum of data centers and the integration of LS Power are creating a new earnings profile in which the valuation multiple cannot remain at traditional levels.

In this context, the valuation for 2026 can be described through three realistic scenarios.

In the bullish scenario, EPS are $10-$11, driven by accelerated new contracts, high pricing of long-term agreements and the direct contribution of LS Power.

Such a level of earnings justifies a P/E of 24 and 26x, as the market recognizes a new growth profile that combines energy, technology and stable flows.

The corresponding share price is between $240 and $285 (up to +70%) which turns NRG into a pure AI-infrastructure growth story.

In the basic case, EPS are $8.2-$8.6 per share. This range incorporates smooth progress on BYOP projects, steady growth in retail and smooth integration of LS Power.

The P/E is 19-21x at a valuation that balances stable cash flows and increasing exposure to data centers.

The share price is $155 to $180, with slight upside from the current levels and stabilizing trends to absorb the high 2025 performance.

In the bearish scenario, EPS are $7-$7.3 per share due to delays or slower contract development.

The P/E is 15-17x which brings the stock at $105-$120 (-25%). As I see it, the downside remains manageable thanks to LS Power's stable cash flows and added value.

The overall picture shows that NRG clearly has an upward asymmetry. The bear case has limited depth while the base and bull cases offer substantial prospects for the stock to be repositioned at a higher valuation, in an environment where energy is becoming the critical pillar of artificial intelligence.

The stock is following a long-term upward trend and according to the above analysis, it has climbed from the $30 area (two years ago) to 165.

Even more interesting than the positive performance itself is the qualitative way it was recorded, with few pullbacks and controlled movement.

For the continuation, support is at 149, with the next technical target at 215 resulting from the range of the fluctuation.

Risks

As all Companies, NRG has its own risks.

The first is the the regulatory environment in ERCOT, PJM and other competitive electricity markets.

Changes in capacity mechanisms, capacity auctions or regulatory price interventions may affect future returns.

A second one is related to he execution of the data center strategy.Large-scale projects require time-consuming interconnection, licensing and construction of new units.

Delays in interconnection queues or Texas Energy Fund projects can shift revenues and pressure valuations.

In addition, hyperscalers have strong bargaining power and can change or shift their investment plans.

Third, the LS Power acquisition embodies classic integration risk.

The combination of two large production portfolios requires sound organization, integration of trading, risk management and unit maintenance.

Any delays in leveraging synergies or unexpected costs could limit the expected increase in profits.

Fourth, the company remains sensitive to interest rate movements and spreads as it finances significant investments through debt.

Refinancing old, low-interest issues with more expensive money partially reduces the benefit from new projects.

Correspondingly, negative surprises in natural gas or electricity prices could affect margins, despite hedging programs.

Finally, in the retail segment, competition remains intense, especially in states where consumers compare directly with regulated tariffs.

Further margin compression in these markets should be offset by the strengthening of the Texan portfolio and Smart Home.

Conclusion

NRG is in a rare position where long term market forces are clearly working in its favor.

Demand for power, particularly from data centers, is growing faster than supply in key competitive markets.

The company has already demonstrated that it can translate these trends into tangible financial results and rising guidance.

The addition of the LS Power portfolio, 5.4 GW pipeline and Smart Home ecosystem further strengthen the investment narrative.

Given its growth profile, strong cash flow and consistent capital return program, the stock justifies a "BUY" recommendation for investors seeking exposure to the next wave of the U.S. energy market.

data.title: Alger Concentrated Equity ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • During the third quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary. The largest sector overweight was Utilities and the largest sector underweight was Information Technology.
  • The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Industrials and Health Care were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

In the third quarter of 2025, U.S. equity markets extended their upward trajectory with the S&P 500 Index up 8.12%. Strong equity market performance was propelled by a combination of improving economic conditions, solid corporate earnings, and growing expectations for monetary easing. A key driver was the shift in Federal Reserve (Fed) policy outlook: what began as a cautious stance going into the third quarter evolved into a more dovish tone as signs of labor market softening emerged. Fed Chair Powell’s remarks at Jackson Hole reinforced this pivot, and the September 25-basis-point rate cut marked the resumption of monetary easing. While uncertainty remained about the pace of additional cuts, the prospect of lower rates supported equity valuations and investor sentiment. At the same time, inflation—though still above the Fed’s 2% target— showed signs of moderation, allowing markets to focus on the likelihood of policy support rather than restrictive measures.

Corporate earnings provided another strong tailwind, as second-quarter results broadly exceeded expectations, and third quarter guidance remained upbeat despite some tariff- related cost pressures. Many firms successfully mitigated these headwinds through pricing strategies and operational efficiencies, while consumer spending stayed firm, buoyed by steady gains in household net worth and greater clarity around trade tariffs. Within the S&P 500 Index, the Information Technology sector outperformed, driven by continued enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI), along with robust demand for cloud and semiconductor products. In contrast, the Consumer Staples sector underperformed the index. While consumer resilience supported market optimism, there was growing acknowledgment that lower-income households are under significant strain, and even higher-income families are feeling pressure, as reflected in increased ‘trade-down’ shopping behavior.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.
